stem: Ninety minutes after his second dose of intramuscular haloperidol for agitation, a 19-year-old man develops a painful, sustained upward and lateral deviation of both eyes, clenching of the jaw, and twisting of the neck. He is frightened but protecting his airway. Which agent, given intramuscularly, will most rapidly reverse this reaction?
explanation: This is an acute dystonic reaction (oculogyric crisis with jaw and neck involvement) caused by D2 blockade acutely unbalancing striatal acetylcholine against dopamine; an intramuscular/intravenous central anticholinergic rebalances it within minutes, making benztropine the rescue agent. Amantadine is a dopaminergic/NMDA agent for drug-induced parkinsonism, not an acute-dystonia rescue, and works orally and slowly. Valbenazine is a VMAT2 inhibitor that further depletes dopamine (used for tardive dyskinesia, and US-only) — the wrong direction for a dystonia driven by dopamine blockade. Propranolol is first-line for akathisia, not for dystonia.

source.name: Benztropine product monograph — acute dystonia / drug-induced EPS

stem: A 34-year-old woman started on risperidone two weeks ago cannot stop moving — she paces constantly, rocks whenever she sits, and describes an unbearable inner restlessness she rates as worse than her psychosis. A trial of an oral anticholinergic gave no relief. She has no asthma, bradycardia, or heart block. Which agent is first-line for her condition?
explanation: Her restlessness is antipsychotic-induced akathisia, for which a non-selective beta-blocker is first-line; the failed anticholinergic trial is a classic clue, because anticholinergics do not reliably relieve akathisia. Trihexyphenidyl is one such anticholinergic (useful for acute dystonia and parkinsonism, not akathisia, and it carries euphoriant misuse potential). Prazosin is an α1 antagonist for PTSD nightmares, not akathisia. Dexmedetomidine is an α2-agonist for acute agitation — a different target from the drug-induced restlessness of akathisia.
focus: antipsychotic-induced akathisia → propranolol (anticholinergics do not help)

stem: A 62-year-old man treated with a first-generation antipsychotic for over a decade develops persistent involuntary chewing, lip-smacking, and writhing tongue movements that continue even at rest. You practise in Canada and want an agent that will actually reduce the movements rather than worsen them. Which is the appropriate choice?
explanation: These orofacial-lingual movements are tardive dyskinesia, driven by dopamine-receptor hypersensitivity after chronic D2 blockade; the treatment is a VMAT2 inhibitor, which depletes presynaptic dopamine and reduces the movements. In Canada the available VMAT2 inhibitor is tetrabenazine (Nitoman). Deutetrabenazine is also a VMAT2 inhibitor but is US-only (Austedo), so it is not the Canadian choice. Procyclidine and benztropine are anticholinergics — they treat acute dystonia and parkinsonism but WORSEN tardive dyskinesia by further unmasking dopamine hypersensitivity.
focus: tardive dyskinesia → VMAT2 inhibitor (Canada-available tetrabenazine; anticholinergics worsen TD)

source.name: Tetrabenazine (Nitoman) monograph — tardive dyskinesia; VMAT2 class

stem: A 41-year-old veteran with PTSD is stable on his SSRI by day but is jolted awake most nights by vivid combat nightmares, drenched in sweat with his heart pounding. He has no daytime hypotension. Which bedtime agent most specifically targets the trauma-related nightmares?
explanation: PTSD nightmares reflect nocturnal noradrenergic surges; a bedtime α1-adrenergic antagonist blunts this and is the off-label go-to, so prazosin is correct (start low at bedtime and warn about the first-dose orthostatic hypotension). Propranolol is a beta-blocker used for akathisia, tremor, and adrenergic arousal, but it is not the specific nightmare agent and can itself cause vivid dreams and nightmares. Benztropine is an anticholinergic for EPS with no role here. Armodafinil is a wakefulness-promoting agent that would fragment sleep further — the wrong direction entirely.

source.name: Prazosin monograph — α1 antagonism, first-dose orthostatic hypotension; PTSD-nightmare use

stem: A 27-year-old with polysomnography-confirmed narcolepsy has irresistible daytime sleep attacks that disrupt her job despite good sleep hygiene. Working in a Canadian clinic, you want to start an approved wakefulness-promoting agent. Which do you prescribe?
explanation: The excessive daytime sleepiness of narcolepsy is treated with a wakefulness-promoting agent; modafinil is approved and available in Canada for this indication (counsel about reduced oral-contraceptive efficacy from its CYP3A4 induction). Armodafinil is modafinil's longer-acting R-enantiomer but is US-only (Nuvigil), so it is not the Canadian choice. Amantadine is used for fatigue and drug-induced parkinsonism, not narcoleptic sleep attacks. Dexmedetomidine is a sedating α2-agonist for acute agitation — the opposite of a wakefulness agent.
focus: excessive daytime sleepiness / narcolepsy → modafinil (Canada-approved wakefulness agent)

source.name: Modafinil product monograph — narcolepsy; CYP3A4 induction / contraceptive caveat
